design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect hidden script pages across themed movie studio levels while managing character placement and puzzle-solving through object interaction and character-swapping mechanics
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing exploration for hidden scripts against linear stage progression; managing three characters with different utility and positioning (stacking mechanic)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Navigate the Warner Brothers studio as the iconic Animaniacs trio, solving environmental puzzles and discovering hidden collectibles across cinematic movie parody stages
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: SNES version deliberately shifts from Genesis's straightforward platforming toward a collectathon with heavy emphasis on hidden script pages (24 total), puzzle-solving, and character synergy; no traditional health system—defeat immediately sends characters to rescue stages; rewards 100% collection with superior ending; distinctly less action-focused than contemporary platformers

difficulty.lives_system: No traditional lives; characters are individually defeated/captured and must be rescued via dedicated rescue stages; game ends when all three are eliminated
difficulty.checkpointing: Rescue stages serve as dynamic checkpoints triggered by character defeat; no explicit save system documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Slot machine system provides risk-reward currency management (coins activate slot for power-ups like temporary invincibility, character revival, or coin modification); script collection is completely optional with three-tier endings based on completion percentage

progression.upgrade_system: Slot machine currency system: collect coins to activate pseudo-random power-ups (invincibility, character recovery, coin gain/loss); stacking mechanic allows two or three characters to reach elevated platforms with variable power
progression.unlock_mechanism: Final stage (Editing Room) unlocks only after completing all other stages; boss rush section precedes final Pinky and the Brain encounter
replayability_hooks: Three distinct endings tied to script page collection percentage (incomplete, partial, perfect)
24 hidden script pages scattered across stages in hard-to-reach and secret locations (player-initiated actions required to reveal some)
Character-swapping puzzle design encourages multiple approaches to same environments
Slot machine RNG adds variance to power-up acquisition on repeated plays

world_building_techniques: Movie genre parodies embedded in stage design and visual themes
Script page collection as narrative objective (film production framing)
Film memorabilia and studio setting as cohesive world context
Character interaction requirements to reveal hidden scripts (environmental storytelling through puzzle gates)

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters tied to stage-specific themes; defeat requires use of themed stage objects rather than direct combat attributes; boss rush mechanic in final stage remixes previous encounters
notable_bosses: Pinky and the Brain (final encounter after boss rush)
Stage-specific bosses (names not detailed in sources but framed as defeatable through environmental interaction rather than character attacks)

faction_indicators: Pinky and the Brain as antagonists (world domination plot, script theft motivation)
Warner Bros. studio authority structure (CEO delegation of recovery mission to siblings)
Small robots working for Pinky and the Brain (minion faction)
danger_signals: Ralph the Security Guard as persistent threat (captures characters, forces rescue stage activation)
Harmful enemies and obstacles throughout stages
Script page scarcity creates implicit stakes around film completion
environmental_storytelling: Movie set parodies convey genre context and visual coherence
Script pages hidden in thematic locations tied to stage content
Studio lot geography reinforces institutional setting and authority structures

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Animaniacs SNES article)
